examples.da: Jeg skal købe nyt tøj til vinteren.
examples.en: I need to buy new clothes for the winter.

examples.da: Hendes tøj ligger på gulvet.
examples.en: Her clothes are lying on the floor.

tøj.gotcha: Uncountable: no plural for 'clothes'. It is et-gender: 'tøjet', not 'tøjen'.

examples.da: Bo kommer til festen i aften.
examples.en: Bo is coming to the party tonight.

Bo.gotcha: Identical to the verb 'bo' (to live); capitalization distinguishes the name.

examples.da: Vi bor i København.
examples.en: We live in Copenhagen.

examples.da: Hvor længe har du boet her?
examples.en: How long have you lived here?

bo.gotcha: 'Bo' means to reside somewhere; use 'leve' for being alive.

examples.da: Hva, sagde du noget?
examples.en: What, did you say something?

hva.gotcha: Informal spoken form of 'hvad'. Use 'hvad' in writing.

examples.da: Vil du have en kop kaffe?
examples.en: Would you like a cup of coffee?

examples.da: Jeg drikker kaffe hver morgen.
examples.en: I drink coffee every morning.

examples.da: Hun sidder ved vinduet.
examples.en: She is sitting by the window.

examples.da: Vi sad og talte hele aftenen.
examples.en: We sat and talked all evening.

sidde.gotcha: Irregular past: sad, har siddet. Don't confuse with 'sætte' (to set/put).

examples.da: Godmorgen! Sov du godt?
examples.en: Good morning! Did you sleep well?

examples.da: Han er nede i kælderen.
examples.en: He is down in the basement.

nede.gotcha: 'Nede' is location (being down); 'ned' is movement (going down).

examples.da: Frank arbejder på et kontor i byen.
examples.en: Frank works at an office in town.

examples.da: Lykke er min bedste veninde.
examples.en: Lykke is my best friend.

Lykke.gotcha: Also the common noun 'lykke' (happiness); capitalization distinguishes the name.

examples.da: Penge kan ikke købe lykke.
examples.en: Money cannot buy happiness.

examples.da: Held og lykke med eksamen!
examples.en: Good luck with the exam!

examples.da: Han kommer givet for sent igen.
examples.en: He will undoubtedly be late again.

givet.gotcha: Also the past participle of 'give' (to give); context decides the meaning.

examples.da: Katten gemmer sig derinde i skabet.
examples.en: The cat is hiding in there in the closet.

examples.da: Jamen, det er jo en god idé!
examples.en: Well, that's a good idea!

jamen.gotcha: Not 'yes' + 'men'; a filler expressing surprise or mild protest, like 'well, but'.

examples.da: Det var en dum fejl.
examples.en: That was a stupid mistake.

examples.da: Vær ikke så dum!
examples.en: Don't be so silly!

examples.da: Jeg er syg og bliver hjemme i dag.
examples.en: I am sick and staying home today.

syg.gotcha: In slang 'syg' can mean 'crazy/awesome', like English 'sick'.

examples.da: Du kan søde teen med honning.
examples.en: You can sweeten the tea with honey.

søde.gotcha: Looks like the adjective 'sød' (sweet/cute) in plural or definite form; context decides.

examples.da: De bor i et kæmpe hus.
examples.en: They live in a huge house.

examples.da: Vi må kæmpe for vores rettigheder.
examples.en: We must fight for our rights.

kæmpe.gotcha: Same word is adjective (huge), noun (giant) and verb (to fight).

examples.da: Hvilket mærke er din bil?
examples.en: What brand is your car?

examples.da: Kan du mærke, hvor koldt det er?
examples.en: Can you feel how cold it is?

mærke.gotcha: As a verb it means 'to feel/notice' — a very common meaning not obvious from the noun.

examples.da: Læreren skal rette vores opgaver.
examples.en: The teacher has to grade our assignments.

examples.da: Jeg vil gerne rette en fejl.
examples.en: I would like to correct a mistake.

examples.da: Bonden har hundrede svin på gården.
examples.en: The farmer has a hundred pigs on the farm.

svin.gotcha: Plural is also 'svin' (unchanged). Often used as an insult, like 'swine'.

examples.da: Fiskeren satte et lod på snøren.
examples.en: The fisherman put a sinker on the line.

lod.gotcha: Also the past tense of 'lade' (to let): 'han lod mig gå' = 'he let me go'.

examples.da: Skal vi se en film i aften?
examples.en: Shall we watch a movie tonight?

film.gotcha: Plural is also 'film' (unchanged): 'tre film', not 'tre films'.

examples.da: Desværre kan jeg ikke komme i morgen.
examples.en: Unfortunately, I can't come tomorrow.

examples.da: Landet har været i krig i tre år.
examples.en: The country has been at war for three years.

examples.da: Vælg et tal mellem en og ti.
examples.en: Pick a number between one and ten.

tal.gotcha: Plural is also 'tal' (unchanged). Also the imperative of 'tale' (speak): 'Tal højere!'

examples.da: Jeg skal skrive en mail til min chef.
examples.en: I have to write an email to my boss.

examples.da: De har et godt forhold til deres naboer.
examples.en: They have a good relationship with their neighbors.

examples.da: Under de forhold kunne vi ikke arbejde.
examples.en: Under those conditions we could not work.

forhold.gotcha: Plural is also 'forhold' (unchanged). Means both 'relationship' and 'conditions/circumstances'.

examples.da: Stormen gjorde stor skade på huset.
examples.en: The storm did great damage to the house.

examples.da: Rygning skader dit helbred.
examples.en: Smoking harms your health.

skade.gotcha: Also the bird 'magpie' — same spelling, different word.

examples.da: Toilettet for herrer er til venstre.
examples.en: The men's restroom is on the left.

herre.gotcha: In slang, 'herre' is an intensifier: 'herre godt' = 'really good'.

examples.da: Han sad i fængsel i fem år.
examples.en: He was in prison for five years.

fængsel.gotcha: Et-gender: 'et fængsel, fængslet'. Note the e drops in definite/plural forms.

examples.da: Vi mødes klokken syv i morgen.
examples.en: We meet at seven o'clock tomorrow.

syv.gotcha: Pronounced with soft v, roughly 'syw' — the y-sound trips up English speakers.

examples.da: Godnat, sov godt!
examples.en: Good night, sleep well!

examples.da: Han lærte at skyde med bue og pil.
examples.en: He learned to shoot with a bow and arrow.

examples.da: Kan du skyde bordet ind mod væggen?
examples.en: Can you push the table against the wall?

skyde.gotcha: Irregular past: skød, har skudt. 'Skyde skylden på' = 'blame someone'.

examples.da: Politiet fandt et lig i skoven.
examples.en: The police found a body in the forest.

examples.da: To plus to er lig fire.
examples.en: Two plus two equals four.

lig.gotcha: As a noun it means 'corpse' — startling next to the adjective sense 'equal'.

examples.da: Du kan bare følge efter mig.
examples.en: You can just follow me.

examples.da: Beslutningen fik store følger.
examples.en: The decision had major consequences.

følge.gotcha: Irregular past: fulgte, har fulgt. The g is soft/nearly silent.

examples.da: Goddag, hvad kan jeg hjælpe med?
examples.en: Good day, how can I help you?

goddag.gotcha: Fairly formal; 'hej' is the everyday greeting.

examples.da: Vores hund elsker at gå tur.
examples.en: Our dog loves going for walks.

hund.gotcha: The d is silent: pronounced roughly 'hoon'.

examples.da: Hun blev vinder af konkurrencen.
examples.en: She became the winner of the competition.

vinder.gotcha: Also the present tense of 'vinde' (to win): 'han vinder altid'.

examples.da: Det var et klogt træk i spillet.
examples.en: That was a clever move in the game.

examples.da: Luk vinduet, der er træk herinde.
examples.en: Close the window, there's a draft in here.

træk.gotcha: Also means 'a draft' (cold air) — a very common everyday sense.

examples.da: Byen ser helt anderledes ud om vinteren.
examples.en: The town looks completely different in winter.

anderledes.gotcha: Same form as adjective and adverb; it never inflects.

examples.da: Så du fodboldkampen i går?
examples.en: Did you see the football match yesterday?

examples.da: Det var en hård kamp mod sygdommen.
examples.en: It was a hard fight against the illness.

kamp.gotcha: Not English 'camp' — that is 'lejr' in Danish.

examples.da: Vi går en tur, uanset vejret.
examples.en: We are going for a walk, regardless of the weather.

examples.da: Det var blot en misforståelse.
examples.en: It was merely a misunderstanding.

blot.gotcha: Somewhat formal; in speech 'bare' or 'kun' is more common.

examples.da: Husk at drikke vand hele dagen.
examples.en: Remember to drink water all day.

examples.da: De så en vild hest på marken.
examples.en: They saw a wild horse in the field.

examples.da: Festen var helt vild!
examples.en: The party was totally crazy!

vild.gotcha: 'At være vild med' means 'to be crazy about', i.e. to really like.

examples.da: Kom, vi må afsted nu!
examples.en: Come on, we have to leave now!

afsted.gotcha: Often written 'af sted' (two words); both spellings are accepted.

examples.da: Vi holder fest på lørdag.
examples.en: We are having a party on Saturday.

fest.gotcha: You 'holde' (hold) a party in Danish, not 'have' one.

examples.da: Tillykke med fødselsdagen!
examples.en: Happy birthday!

tillykke.gotcha: Always used with 'med': 'tillykke med...' — even for birthdays.

examples.da: Drej til venstre ved kirken.
examples.en: Turn left at the church.

venstre.gotcha: Capitalized 'Venstre' is confusingly a right-of-centre political party.

examples.da: Kan du passe børnene i aften?
examples.en: Can you look after the kids tonight?

examples.da: Jakken passer perfekt.
examples.en: The jacket fits perfectly.

passe.gotcha: Not English 'pass' — means look after, fit, or be correct. 'Pas på!' = 'watch out!'

examples.da: Hun læser en bog hver uge.
examples.en: She reads a book every week.

examples.da: Han læser medicin på universitetet.
examples.en: He studies medicine at university.

læse.gotcha: Also means 'to study' a subject at university, not just 'to read'.

examples.da: Billetten kostede halvtreds dollars.
examples.en: The ticket cost fifty dollars.

examples.da: Barnet sad på sin mors skød.
examples.en: The child sat on its mother's lap.

skød.gotcha: Also the past tense of 'skyde' (to shoot): 'han skød' = 'he shot'.

examples.da: Hun blev meget vred over nyheden.
examples.en: She became very angry about the news.

vred.gotcha: Also the past tense of 'vride' (to twist/wring).

examples.da: Du kan altid stole på mig.
examples.en: You can always rely on me.

stole.gotcha: The verb only exists as 'stole på' (trust). Also the plural of 'stol' (chair).

examples.da: Børnene går i skole klokken otte.
examples.en: The children go to school at eight o'clock.

examples.da: Mike er lige flyttet til Danmark.
examples.en: Mike has just moved to Denmark.

examples.da: Tal lidt højere, jeg kan ikke høre dig.
examples.en: Speak a little louder, I can't hear you.

examples.da: Hun læste historien højt for børnene.
examples.en: She read the story aloud to the children.

højt.gotcha: 'At læse højt' means to read aloud, not to read loudly.

examples.da: Banken ligger på højre side af gaden.
examples.en: The bank is on the right side of the street.

højre.gotcha: Only means the direction; 'right' as in 'correct' is 'rigtig'.

examples.da: Jeg er bekymret for min gamle far.
examples.en: I am worried about my old father.

examples.da: Michael bor i Aarhus med sin familie.
examples.en: Michael lives in Aarhus with his family.

examples.da: Vil du gøre mig en tjeneste?
examples.en: Will you do me a favor?

examples.da: Tja, det ved jeg ikke rigtig.
examples.en: Well, I don't really know.

tja.gotcha: Not a clear 'yes' — signals hesitation or doubt, unlike 'ja'.

examples.da: Der går ingen bus hertil om søndagen.
examples.en: No bus comes here on Sundays.

examples.da: Kom herind med det samme — straks!
examples.en: Come in here right now — immediately!

examples.da: Jeg ringer tilbage straks efter mødet.
examples.en: I'll call back right after the meeting.

examples.da: Min onkel bor på landet.
examples.en: My uncle lives in the countryside.

examples.da: Kan du åbne vinduet, tak?
examples.en: Can you open the window, please?

examples.da: Butikken åbner klokken ti.
examples.en: The shop opens at ten o'clock.

åbne.gotcha: The adjective 'open' is 'åben'; the verb is 'åbne'.

examples.da: Min far gav mig et godt råd.
examples.en: My father gave me a good piece of advice.

examples.da: Vi har ikke råd til en ny bil.
examples.en: We can't afford a new car.

råd.gotcha: 'Have råd til' means 'to afford'. A single tip is 'et råd'; plural is also 'råd'.

examples.da: Min telefon er løbet tør for strøm.
examples.en: My phone has run out of battery.

examples.da: Fru Hansen bor på anden sal.
examples.en: Mrs. Hansen lives on the second floor.

fru.gotcha: Only used before a name; sounds old-fashioned in modern Danish.

examples.da: Han er en frisk lille knægt.
examples.en: He is a lively little lad.

knægt.gotcha: The g is soft; also the jack in a deck of cards.

examples.da: Jeg løber en tur hver morgen.
examples.en: I go for a run every morning.

examples.da: Tiden løber hurtigt.
examples.en: Time passes quickly.

examples.da: Mødet starter klokken otte.
examples.en: The meeting starts at eight o'clock.

otte.gotcha: Pronounced 'oh-de' with soft d, not like English 'otter'.

examples.da: Kan du sende mig adressen?
examples.en: Can you send me the address?

sende.gotcha: Past tense is 'sendte' (with t), pronounced like 'sende'.

examples.da: Hun har langt lyst hår.
examples.en: She has long blond hair.

hår.gotcha: Plural is also 'hår' (unchanged). Don't confuse with 'år' (year).

examples.da: Kan du forklare, hvordan det virker?
examples.en: Can you explain how it works?

examples.da: Det er dejligt vejr i dag.
examples.en: The weather is lovely today.

examples.da: Hvor dejligt at se dig!
examples.en: How lovely to see you!

dejligt.gotcha: The g is silent: pronounced roughly 'die-lit'.

examples.da: Hun arbejder på et kontor i centrum.
examples.en: She works at an office downtown.

kontor.gotcha: You are 'på kontoret' (on the office), not 'i'.

examples.da: Javel, kaptajn! Vi sejler nu.
examples.en: Aye aye, captain! We're sailing now.

javel.gotcha: Military or ironic tone; sounds odd as an everyday 'yes'.

examples.da: Hun er for ung til at køre bil.
examples.en: She is too young to drive a car.

examples.da: Må jeg tage et billede af jer?
examples.en: May I take a picture of you?

billede.gotcha: Both d's are essentially silent: pronounced roughly 'bill-uh'.

examples.da: Hun leder en stor afdeling.
examples.en: She manages a large department.

examples.da: Jeg leder efter mine nøgler.
examples.en: I am looking for my keys.

lede.gotcha: 'Lede efter' means 'to look for' — very common and different from 'to lead'.

examples.da: Politimanden trak sin pistol.
examples.en: The police officer drew his pistol.

pistol.gotcha: Stress on the last syllable: pi-STOL.

examples.da: Peter og jeg har kendt hinanden i årevis.
examples.en: Peter and I have known each other for years.

examples.da: David spiller guitar i et band.
examples.en: David plays guitar in a band.

examples.da: Politiet efterforsker et mord i byen.
examples.en: The police are investigating a murder in town.

mord.gotcha: The d is silent: pronounced like 'mor' (mother).

examples.da: Hun er et godt menneske.
examples.en: She is a good person.

examples.da: Der var mange mennesker på gaden.
examples.en: There were many people in the street.

menneske.gotcha: Et-gender despite meaning a person: 'et menneske'. Plural 'mennesker' = people.

examples.da: Fandens! Jeg har glemt mine nøgler.
examples.en: Damn it! I've forgotten my keys.

fandens.gotcha: A mild-to-medium swear word; avoid in polite or formal settings.

examples.da: Politiet fandt spor i sneen.
examples.en: The police found tracks in the snow.

examples.da: Toget kører fra spor to.
examples.en: The train leaves from platform two.

spor.gotcha: Plural is also 'spor' (unchanged). 'Ikke spor' means 'not at all'.

examples.da: Skal vi dele en pizza?
examples.en: Shall we share a pizza?

examples.da: Vi delte regningen mellem os.
examples.en: We split the bill between us.

dele.gotcha: Also the plural of the noun 'del' (part): 'to dele' = 'two parts'.

examples.da: Vi bor for enden af vejen.
examples.en: We live at the end of the road.

examples.da: Hvordan vil filmen ende?
examples.en: How will the movie end?

ende.gotcha: Colloquially also means 'behind/bottom' (body part) — context matters.

examples.da: Jeg låner en bog på biblioteket.
examples.en: I am borrowing a book from the library.

bog.gotcha: Irregular plural: bøger. The g is soft, roughly 'bow'.

examples.da: Pisk hviderne til de er stive.
examples.en: Whisk the egg whites until stiff.

hvide.gotcha: Also the definite/plural form of the adjective 'hvid' (white). The h is silent.

examples.da: Er det ægte guld?
examples.en: Is that real gold?

examples.da: Hun viste ægte glæde over gaven.
examples.en: She showed genuine joy over the gift.

examples.da: Jeg håber, vores hold vinder i aften.
examples.en: I hope our team wins tonight.

vinde.gotcha: Irregular past: vandt, har vundet. Don't confuse with 'vinden' (the wind).

examples.da: Hun er doktor i fysik.
examples.en: She has a doctorate in physics.

doktor.gotcha: The everyday word for a physician is 'læge'; 'doktor' sounds academic or old-fashioned.

examples.da: Hvilket dyr kan du bedst lide?
examples.en: Which animal do you like best?

examples.da: Den jakke er alt for dyr.
examples.en: That jacket is far too expensive.

dyr.gotcha: Same word means 'animal' (noun) and 'expensive' (adjective). Plural of the noun is 'dyr'.

examples.da: Kom indenfor, det regner!
examples.en: Come inside, it's raining!

examples.da: Hun arbejder indenfor IT.
examples.en: She works within IT.

examples.da: Vær stille, babyen sover.
examples.en: Be quiet, the baby is sleeping.

sover.gotcha: Usually the present tense of 'sove' (to sleep); the noun 'sleeper' is rare.

examples.da: Du bør gå til lægen med den hoste.
examples.en: You should see the doctor about that cough.

læge.gotcha: Don't confuse with 'læse' (to read) — the soft g vs s trips up learners.

examples.da: Maden står på bordet.
examples.en: The food is on the table.

bord.gotcha: The d is silent: pronounced roughly 'bor'. Not English 'board'.

examples.da: Hun er den bedste i klassen.
examples.en: She is the best in the class.

examples.da: Jeg kan bedst lide sommeren.
examples.en: I like summer the best.

examples.da: Han faldt på røven på isen.
examples.en: He fell on his backside on the ice.

røv.gotcha: Vulgar — fine among friends, rude in polite company.

examples.da: Jeg elsker dig.
examples.en: I love you.

examples.da: Hun elsker at rejse.
examples.en: She loves to travel.

elske.gotcha: Strong word; for liking people casually Danes say 'kan godt lide'.

examples.da: Tænd lyset, det er mørkt herinde.
examples.en: Turn on the light, it's dark in here.

examples.da: Hun har lyst hår.
examples.en: She has fair hair.

lys.gotcha: Also means 'candle': 'stearinlys'. Don't confuse with 'lyst' (desire): 'have lyst til'.

examples.da: Filmen begynder om ti minutter.
examples.en: The movie starts in ten minutes.

examples.da: Ha! Jeg vidste, du tog fejl.
examples.en: Ha! I knew you were wrong.

ha.gotcha: Also informal spelling of 'have': "vil du ha' en kop?"

examples.da: Vi skal sikre, at alle er informeret.
examples.en: We must ensure that everyone is informed.

sikre.gotcha: 'Sikre sig' means 'to make sure'; also plural of the adjective 'sikker' (safe/sure).

examples.da: Han måtte forlade landet.
examples.en: He had to leave the country.

examples.da: Forlad mig, det var ikke min mening.
examples.en: Forgive me, I didn't mean it.

forlade.gotcha: 'Forlade sig på' means 'to rely on' — quite different from 'to leave'.

examples.da: Du bør tale med en advokat først.
examples.en: You should talk to a lawyer first.

advokat.gotcha: Not 'advocate' in the general sense — specifically a lawyer.

examples.da: Vi bliver nødt til at ændre planen.
examples.en: We have to change the plan.

examples.da: Jeg så en god film i aftes.
examples.en: I watched a good movie last night.

aftes.gotcha: Only used in the phrase 'i aftes' (yesterday evening); never stands alone.

examples.da: Vores lærer er meget dygtig.
examples.en: Our teacher is very skilled.

lærer.gotcha: Also the present tense of 'lære' (to learn/teach): 'jeg lærer dansk'.

examples.da: De har lige fået en baby.
examples.en: They just had a baby.

examples.da: Hun er en kendt skuespiller i Danmark.
examples.en: She is a famous actress in Denmark.

kendt.gotcha: Also the past participle of 'kende' (to know).

examples.da: Jeg vil ikke dumme mig til festen.
examples.en: I don't want to make a fool of myself at the party.

dumme.gotcha: Always reflexive: 'dumme sig'. Also the definite/plural form of 'dum' (stupid).

examples.da: Har du overhovedet læst bogen?
examples.en: Have you even read the book?

examples.da: Det gør ikke ondt overhovedet.
examples.en: It doesn't hurt at all.

overhovedet.gotcha: One word — not 'over hovedet' (over the head).

examples.da: Hun har en sort kjole på.
examples.en: She is wearing a black dress.

sort.gotcha: False friend: means 'black', not English 'sort/kind' (that is 'slags').

examples.da: Hun var helt ene hjemme.
examples.en: She was all alone at home.

ene.gotcha: 'Alene' is the normal word for 'alone'; 'ene' is mostly in fixed phrases.

examples.da: Min søster bor i USA.
examples.en: My sister lives in the USA.

USA.gotcha: Pronounced letter by letter in Danish: 'oo-ess-ah'.

examples.da: Alle kalder ham Thomas.
examples.en: Everyone calls him Thomas.

examples.da: Hvad kalder man det på dansk?
examples.en: What do you call that in Danish?

kalde.gotcha: Only for naming. To phone someone is 'ringe til', never 'kalde'.

examples.da: Vi spiser middag klokken seks.
examples.en: We eat dinner at six o'clock.

examples.da: Butikken lukker klokken tolv middag.
examples.en: The shop closes at twelve noon.

middag.gotcha: Means both 'noon' and 'dinner' (the evening meal) — despite literally being 'midday'.

examples.da: Det er varmt herinde.
examples.en: It is warm in here.

examples.da: James kommer fra England.
examples.en: James is from England.
